title: Paired Muscle Relaxation, Step by Step If you have decided to practice paired muscle relaxation, it can be very helpful to practice relaxing each of your muscles first.

pages.text:
331
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 6b (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 4a)
Paired Muscle Relaxation, Step by Step
If you have decided to practice paired muscle relaxation, it can be very helpful to practice
relaxing each of your muscles first.
When you are starting, practice in a quiet place to reduce distractions, and make sure that you
have enough time. As you improve with practice, you will want to practice in many different kinds of
places, so that you can relax effectively when you most need to.
Remember that effectiveness improves with practice. If judgments arise, observe them, let
them go, and return to your practice. If you become anxious, try focusing on breathing in to the
count of 5 and out to the count of 7 (or the counts you have already determined for paced breathing),
breathing all the while into your belly until you can return to relaxation exercises.
Now that you are ready to begin...
1. Get your body into a comfortable position in which you can relax. Loosen tight clothing. Lie or
sit down, with all body parts uncrossed and no body part supporting any others.
2. For each area of the body listed below, gather tension by tightening muscles. Focus on the
sensation of tightness in and around that area. Hold the tension as you inhale for 5вҖ“6 seconds,
then release and breathe out.
3. As you release, say in your mind very slowly the word вҖңRelax.вҖқ
4. Observe the changes in sensations as you relax for 10вҖ“15 seconds then move on to the next
muscle.
Start first with each of the 16 muscle groups.
Once you can do that, practice with medium groups of muscles and then large groups.
Once you are good at that, practice tensing your entire body at once.
When you tense your entire body, you are like a robotвҖ”stiff, nothing moving.
When you relax your entire body, you are like a rag dollвҖ”all muscles drooping down.
Once you can relax all your muscles, practice three or four times a day until you can routinely
relax your entire body rapidly.
By practicing pairing exhaling and the word вҖңRelaxвҖқ with relaxing your muscles, you will
eventually be able to relax just by letting go and saying the word вҖңRelax.вҖқ
Large
Medium
Small
пЈұпЈҙпЈІпЈҙ пЈі
пЈұ
пЈІ
пЈі 1. Hands and wrists: Make fists with both hands and pull fists up on the wrists. 2.	Lower and upper arms: Make fists and bend both arms up to touch your shoulders. 3.	Shoulders: Pull both shoulders up to your ears.
пЈұ
пЈҙ
пЈҙ
пЈІ
пЈҙ
пЈҙ
пЈі
пЈұпЈҙпЈІпЈҙ пЈі 4. Forehead: Pull eyebrows close together, wrinkling forehead. 5. Eyes: Shut eyes tightly. 6.	Nose and upper cheeks: Scrunch up nose; bring upper lips and cheeks up toward eyes.
пЈұ
пЈІ
пЈі 7. Lips and lower face: Press lips together; bring edges of lips back toward ears. 8.	Tongue and mouth: Teeth together; tongue pushing on upper mouth.
пЈұпЈҙпЈІпЈҙ пЈі 9. Neck: Push head back into chair, floor, or bed, or push chin down to chest.
пЈұ
пЈІ
пЈі
10. Chest: Take deep breath and hold it.
11. Back: Arch back, bringing shoulder blades together.
пЈұ
пЈҙ
пЈҙ
пЈІ
пЈҙ
пЈҙ
пЈі
пЈұ
пЈІ
пЈі
12. Stomach: Hold stomach in tightly.
13. Buttocks: Squeeze buttocks together.
пЈұпЈҙпЈІпЈҙ пЈі
14. Upper legs and thighs: Legs out; tense thighs.
15. Calves: Legs out; point toes down.
16. Ankles: Legs out; point toes together, heels out, toes curled under.
Remember, paired relaxation is a skill. It takes time to develop. With practice, you will notice the benefits.
Note. Adapted from Smith, R. E. (1980). Development of an integrated coping response through cognitiveвҖ“affective stress management train-
ing. In I. G. Sarason & C. D. Spielberger (Eds.), Stress and anxiety (Vol. 7, pp.В 265вҖ“280). Washington, DC: Hemisphere. Copyright 1980 by
Hemisphere Publishing Corporation. Adapted by permission.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Paired Muscle Relaxation, Step by Step If you have decided to practice paired muscle relaxation, it can be very helpful to practice relaxing each of your muscles first.

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
